summary: SWE-ContextBench shows that context reuse in coding agents helps only in a bounded way: short high-quality summaries outperformed both no reuse and full trajectory reuse on this benchmark, but the evidence does not justify a stronger causal claim than that relevance and compression quality matter under the tested setup.

bounded_claims.claim_text: SWE-ContextBench extends SWE-Bench Lite into a context-learning benchmark by pairing 300 base tasks with 99 manually verified related tasks drawn from real issue and pull-request relationships.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports dataset construction and manual verification of task relationships, but not any causal claim about why that structure improves agent performance.

bounded_claims.claim_text: Oracle summary reuse outperformed both no-experience and oracle full-experience reuse, reaching 34.34% resolution versus 26.26% for the baseline and 27.27% for full trajectory reuse.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports a benchmark-specific result pattern and is consistent with the idea that compact, relevant summaries may be more useful than long raw trajectories, but it does not prove that mechanism directly or establish a general law beyond this setup.

bounded_claims.claim_text: The benchmark results suggest that summary quality matters, because free summary reuse performed worse than the no-experience baseline and produced the most patch application failures.
bounded_claims.bounds: The evidence supports a bounded claim that low-quality or poorly matched summaries can degrade performance, but the exact failure pathway remains unresolved; misleading compression, bad retrieval, or downstream patch-generation disruption are all still uncertain.
